title: Tier-2 resonance batch shipped: ROS, Matrix/Mastodon, ANP
content: canon/resonance/ expanded from 6 to 9 pages with the addition of ros.md (the structural pub-sub ancestor at altitude 3 within a single fleet), matrix-and-mastodon.md (the leading federated human-messaging protocols, treated as a class per AMS.md §11.3 framing), and anp.md (the W3C-DID-based three-layer agent network protocol with meta-protocol negotiation). All three follow the resonance template, all cite ams://canon/principles/envelope-altitude-consensus 3x in the standard locations (frontmatter derives_from, body of Where AMS Diverges, Related Canon), all add at least one entrant-specific divergence beyond the shared root, all match the 7-H2-section structure of recursive-mas.md and the Tier-1 batch.

title: Matrix and Mastodon get a single combined resonance page rather than separate pages
content: matrix-and-mastodon.md treats both protocols as one class because (a) AMS.md §11.3 already groups them under "Matrix, Mastodon, and Human Messaging Protocols" with one shared positioning argument, (b) the load-bearing AMS divergence — human-layer assumptions baked into the protocol — applies identically to both, (c) splitting into matrix.md and mastodon.md would duplicate ~80% of the content with no analytical gain. Precedent: recursive-mas.md mentions the latent-comm cluster (Du, Fu, Zheng, Zou) within a single page rather than per-author. Combined-page treatment is reserved for clusters where the AMS-relationship is the same across members; the Tier-1 envelope-altitude entrants got individual pages because the per-entrant divergences were distinct (Agent Cards vs DIDs vs CBOR, etc.).

title: Resonance pages for cooperative-not-competitive protocols still need explicit divergence
content: ros.md is the second resonance page for a protocol AMS deeply borrows from rather than positions against (mcp.md was the first). The page documents alignment as deep — pub-sub primitives, polymorphic subscribers, decoupled lifecycles, late-binding observability are all ROS conventions before they are AMS conventions — and divergence as still mandatory per klappy://canon/resonance. The constraint emerging across mcp.md and ros.md: cooperative protocols earn pages because the resonance convention requires explicit divergence to make the layer relationship legible, not because the protocols compete with AMS. Without the page, the structural-ancestor relationship has no addressable canon surface and future readers might assume AMS originated patterns it actually inherited.

title: The structural ancestor is the sharpest resonance to write
content: Of the nine resonance pages now in canon/resonance/, ros.md was the most generative to write because the alignment is unusually deep at the data-plane level (publishers/subscribers/topics ≈ writers/subscribers/streams) and the divergence is precisely about what changes when scope moves from intra-fleet to cross-organization. The exercise of naming what ROS solved that AMS does not have to solve (typed messages, QoS contracts, master-node discovery, deterministic latency in a single multicast domain) and what AMS solves that ROS structurally cannot (cross-organization reach, instance-independent magic-link addressing, no-registry-at-any-altitude) sharpened the AMS positioning more than any of the agent-comms cluster pages did. Forward implication: when surveying for new resonance candidates, structural ancestors deserve higher priority than competitive contemporaries — the divergence under deep alignment is more informative than the divergence under shallow alignment.

title: canon/resonance/ now has 9 entries spanning four distinct positioning purposes
content: The directory currently covers four distinct kinds of relationship: (1) cooperative-substrate-borrowing — mcp.md (AMS uses MCP for the edge wrapper) and ros.md (AMS inherits pub-sub primitives from ROS); (2) envelope-altitude-cluster contemporaries — agent-messaging-protocol.md, a2a.md, acp.md, nlip.md, anp.md (the 2025–2026 protocol race); (3) considered-and-rejected substrate alternatives — matrix-and-mastodon.md (federated human messaging); (4) altitude-axis alternatives outside the cluster — recursive-mas.md (latent-altitude alternative; opposite end of the altitude axis from NLIP). The four-way classification was not designed; it emerged from the resonance work. Open question for future canon hygiene: whether tagging or sub-directory structure could make the four classes discoverable, or whether the cross-reference graph in each page's Related Canon is sufficient.

title: Next-after-next: the orchestration-frameworks class page and the standing principle candidates
content: The Tier-3 candidate from the survey screening matrix — a single class-page on centralized-orchestration frameworks (LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI) titled something like canon/resonance/centralized-orchestration-frameworks.md — remains as a planned future scope. The framing already exists in HORIZON.md §3 ("the inversion of the currently dominant pattern, where an orchestration agent decides which worker runs when"), so the page would mostly promote that framing into the resonance directory with explicit divergence statements per framework treated as one class. The two standing-principle candidates from the post-batch reflection turn (security-as-subscriber-pattern; the inversion-principle-generalized for klappy.dev upstream) also remain as named-not-urgent items per the prior journal entry. None of these is the next-immediate scope; the operator can let new pressure trigger re-entry (a real security incident, a new entrant making a security claim, or the next time the inversion is the load-bearing argument in a piece of canon being authored).

title: canon/resonance/{ros,matrix-and-mastodon,anp}.md committed
content: Three files committed to canon/resonance/. Total 31,327 bytes for this batch (canon/resonance/ now 86,507 bytes across 9 pages). Each page: full frontmatter (uri ams://canon/resonance/<slug>, title, audience=canon, exposure=nav, tier=3, voice=neutral, stability=stable, tags, epoch=E0008.4, date=2026-05-02, derives_from including ams://canon/principles/envelope-altitude-consensus, governs, status=active); 7 H2 sections matching recursive-mas.md and the Tier-1 batch (AMS Principle, Convergent Quote, Where AMS Aligns, Where AMS Diverges Explicit, Why the Divergence Matters, Operationalization in AMS, Related Canon); each cites ams://canon/principles/envelope-altitude-consensus 3x; each adds at least one entrant-specific divergence beyond the shared root per the constraint encoded in 2026-05-02-ams-envelope-altitude-consensus.tsv. Voice-cliché audited against klappy://canon/constraints/ai-voice-cliches: zero formulaic transitions, zero summary clichés, zero sycophantic openers, zero puffing-importance hits across all three files; em-dash density flags on ros.md and anp.md (para 19 in each) verified as the same bullet-list false-positive pattern as the Tier-1 batch and recursive-mas.md (each em-dash separates a bolded headline from its explanation as distinct semantic work). Cross-reference graph extended: ros.md links to recursive-mas.md (out-of-cluster comparisons sibling pair); matrix-and-mastodon.md links to ros.md (data-plane vs federation-plane structural ancestors); anp.md links to acp.md, a2a.md, and nlip.md (sibling envelope-altitude entrants). The graph is now richer than any index file would be.
